prompt: Extreme close-up of weathered hands opening a brass pocket watch, a folded yellowed letter falls out, warm light, shallow depth of field
subtitle: In the autumn of 1994, a woman opened a dead man's pocket watch and found a letter inside.

prompt: Close-up of faded handwriting on yellowed paper, elegant penmanship, candlelight warmth, the last two lines smudged and unreadable
subtitle: It had been written fifty years earlier, on the night before D-Day, by a young soldier to the woman he loved.

prompt: Young soldier in 1944 uniform folding a letter, then an old man in 1994 holding the same letter, split composition
subtitle: The soldier survived the war. He came home. He married someone else. He never sent the letter. The question is — why?

prompt: Small rural cemetery in Pennsylvania, gray October morning, bare trees, a handful of mourners in dark coats standing around a fresh grave
subtitle: On a gray October morning in 1994, Sophie Bennett stood at her grandfather's grave and realized she had never really known him.

prompt: Sophie standing apart from the small group of mourners, looking hollow and distant, autumn leaves falling around her
subtitle: Thomas Bennett. Eighty years old. A carpenter. A quiet man who built things with his hands and kept everything else locked inside his chest.

prompt: Richard handing Sophie a small key, his face rigid and controlled, the cemetery in soft focus behind them
subtitle: After the service, Richard handed Sophie a key. Someone has to sort through his things.

prompt: Dusty attic filled with old boxes and Army blankets, afternoon light through a small window, Sophie discovering a cigar box
subtitle: Tom's attic smelled of sawdust and decades. Sophie moved through boxes of old tools, faded blueprints, a lifetime of carpentry.

prompt: Close-up of a beautiful brass pocket watch in Sophie's hands, worn smooth, engraved T.B. and E.P. on the back
subtitle: Sophie turned it over. On the back, two sets of initials had been engraved: T.B. & E.P.

prompt: Sophie carefully opening the watch, a tiny folded yellowed letter inside the mechanism, her hands trembling
subtitle: Sophie pressed the clasp. The watch opened. Inside, folded so many times it had become part of the mechanism, was a single sheet of paper.

prompt: Sophie at a farmhouse kitchen table, reading the letter under warm light, the elegant handwriting visible
subtitle: Sophie sat at her grandfather's kitchen table and began to read. The handwriting was elegant. Almost too elegant.

prompt: Close-up of the letter text, beautiful penmanship, then the bottom of the page where ink has bled and smeared beyond reading
subtitle: The last few lines were ruined. The ink had bled and smeared, as if water had been spilled. Or tears.

prompt: Sophie on the phone, shocked expression, Richard's voice stern on the other end, the letter visible on the table
subtitle: Dad, who is Eleanor Parish? — Sophie. Put it back. Don't mention that name again.

prompt: Sophie back in the attic next morning, rain on the roof, finding a black-and-white photograph in an ammunition case
subtitle: Sophie went back to the attic. Inside an old ammunition case — she found a photograph.

prompt: Close-up of a 1944 black-and-white photo: young soldier and nurse smiling at each other, light in their eyes, pencil on back reads Eleanor June 1944 England
subtitle: A young man in uniform standing beside a woman in a nurse's outfit. They were smiling at each other — not at the camera, at each other.

prompt: Richard sitting in the dark with untouched whiskey, Sophie standing in the doorway, tense confrontation
subtitle: That night, Sophie drove to her father's house. Richard was sitting in the dark.

prompt: Close-up of Richard's face cracking with emotion as he speaks Tom's last words
subtitle: He said... Tell her I'm sorry.

prompt: Sophie holding the pocket watch in her pocket, determined expression, making her decision
subtitle: Sophie made her decision. She was going to find Eleanor Parish. She was going to deliver the words her grandfather never said.

prompt: Sophie at National Archives in Washington DC, fluorescent lights, turning through Army service records
subtitle: Sophie started with the National Archives. Tom's Army service record: 29th Infantry Division. Assigned to a field hospital in Dorset.

prompt: Sophie making phone calls, looking frustrated, crossing names off a notepad, dead ends piling up
subtitle: Two weeks of phone calls. Three dead-end leads. One Eleanor Parish in Ohio who turned out to be a schoolteacher born in 1952.

prompt: George Whitfield in a wheelchair in a nursing home, sharp eyes despite aged body, Sophie sitting across from him
subtitle: George Whitfield. Eighty-seven years old. Tom Bennett's closest friend in the 29th Division.

prompt: George smiling warmly, lost in memory, gesturing as he talks about Tom and Eleanor
subtitle: Those two — Tommy and Eleanor — they were the only beautiful thing in that whole ugly war.

prompt: George's face turning somber, pausing mid-sentence
subtitle: D-Day happened.

prompt: George chuckling, demonstrating writing with his hand
subtitle: Your grandfather couldn't write worth a damn — farm boy. Hands made for plows, not pens. I used to write his letters home for him.

prompt: Dramatic Dorset cliffs at sunset June 1944, ships gathering in harbor below, Tom and Eleanor silhouetted on the cliff edge
subtitle: June 4th, 1944. The coast of Dorset. Tom and Eleanor stood on the cliffs above the sea, watching ships gather in the harbor below.

prompt: Tom and Eleanor face to face on the cliffs, his earnest expression, her teasing smile, golden light
subtitle: When this is over, I want to build you a house. With a kitchen big enough to dance in. — You're proposing to me with a kitchen?

prompt: Eleanor pressing the pocket watch back into Tom's hands, her eyes serious and loving
subtitle: Keep it. Bring it back to me. That's your promise.

prompt: Omaha Beach chaos, soldiers wading through water, explosions, Tom hit by shrapnel, medics dragging him
subtitle: The next morning, Tom shipped out to Omaha Beach. He was hit by shrapnel within the first hour.

prompt: Tom lying in a hospital bed in Southampton, bandaged, asking a nurse about Eleanor, the nurse's face falling
subtitle: Where is Eleanor? — I'm sorry, Private. E. Parish is on the casualty list.

prompt: Tom closing his eyes in the hospital bed, rain on the window, all light fading from his expression
subtitle: Tom closed his eyes. And something inside him — the part that planned kitchens big enough to dance in — went quiet forever.

prompt: Sophie standing in a military cemetery on the Dorset coast 1994, white crosses, sea in background, kneeling at a gravestone
subtitle: Sophie stood in a cemetery on the Dorset coast. The casualty list included E. Parish.

prompt: Close-up of the gravestone: Edith Parish 1921-1944, Sophie's shocked expression as she reads
subtitle: Edith Parish. Not Eleanor. Edith. The initial was the same — E. Parish. But it was a different woman entirely.

prompt: Sophie in the British Red Cross archives, surrounded by old documents, discovering Eleanor's survival record
subtitle: The British Red Cross archives confirmed it. Eleanor Parish had survived the Dorset bombing.

prompt: Close-up of an old Red Cross file with a typed note: PFC Thomas J. Bennett — killed in action
subtitle: Eleanor's file: Informed that PFC Thomas J. Bennett was killed in action at Omaha Beach. Except Tom hadn't died.

prompt: Split screen: two clerical forms side by side, E for Edith circled on one, KIA instead of WIA on the other
subtitle: Two clerical errors. Two single letters. Two tiny mistakes in the chaos of the largest military operation in human history.

prompt: Sophie sitting alone in the archive, weeping, surrounded by paper and dust
subtitle: And because of those mistakes, two people who loved each other spent fifty years believing the other was dead.

prompt: Tom in his workshop 1970s, sitting on a stool by the window, holding the pocket watch in both hands, June 6th
subtitle: Every June 6th, Tom closed his workshop early. He would sit by the window and hold the pocket watch in both hands.

prompt: Margaret standing in the workshop doorway holding a coffee cup, watching Tom, then quietly leaving
subtitle: Margaret came to the door with a cup of coffee. She set the cup on the bench and left without a word. She never asked why.

prompt: Eleanor in a 1960s flat in Surrey, setting her keys on a table beside a framed photo of young Tom
subtitle: Three thousand miles away, Eleanor set her keys beside a photograph. A young American soldier. Smiling.

prompt: Eleanor unpacking at a new flat, the photograph being the first thing she places on the mantel
subtitle: She had carried this photograph from posting to posting for twenty years. Every new flat, it was the first thing she unpacked.

prompt: Sophie on a hotel phone in London, crying, Richard's voice on the other end confessing
subtitle: Dad. Eleanor is alive. She's been alive this whole time. — I know.

prompt: Richard's face in shadow, confessing the ten-year secret
subtitle: He found out about ten years ago. Eleanor Parish — alive, living in England. He couldn't break your grandmother's heart to mend his own.

prompt: Sophie looking at the watch, the ticking now urgent, determination in her eyes
subtitle: Tom was gone. But Eleanor was still here. And the letter was still undelivered.

prompt: Sophie on a train heading west through English countryside, green hills and stone walls scrolling past
subtitle: Sophie took a train west from London, watching England scroll past the window like a watercolor painting.

prompt: Sophie standing in the train aisle with her coat, nearly getting off, inner turmoil on her face
subtitle: Halfway through the journey, Sophie nearly got off the train. What if this letter tears open a wound she spent fifty years closing?

prompt: Sophie sitting back down on the train, resolute, the pocket watch visible in her hand
subtitle: She thought of her grandfather's last words. Tell her I'm sorry. Sophie sat back down. She owed them both.

prompt: Charming stone cottage with ivy-covered walls in a Cotswolds village, Sophie walking up the gravel path
subtitle: The cottage was small. Stone walls covered in ivy. A garden that had been loved for decades.

prompt: Sophie knocking on the wooden door, three knocks, then the door opening to reveal Eleanor
subtitle: She knocked. The door opened. And there she was.

prompt: Eleanor in the doorway, 70 years old, white hair, blue eyes, gentle expression, Can I help you dear
subtitle: Eleanor Parish. Seventy years old. White hair pinned back. Eyes the color of the English Channel on a clear day.

prompt: Sophie looking past Eleanor to the photograph on the wall — the same photo of young Tom she found in the attic
subtitle: Over Eleanor's shoulder, on the wall — a photograph. The same photograph Sophie had found in the attic. Fifty years on her wall.

prompt: Eleanor's hand flying to her mouth, eyes filling with tears, recognizing Tom in Sophie's face
subtitle: Oh my God. You have his eyes.

prompt: Sophie and Eleanor sitting in the cozy sitting room, cold tea between them, Sophie placing the letter on the table
subtitle: Sophie told Eleanor everything. Then she pulled out the letter. Carefully. Reverently.

prompt: Eleanor's thin steady fingers unfolding the letter, tears tracking down her cheeks as she reads, then stopping suddenly
subtitle: Eleanor read the first lines. Tears tracked down her cheeks. And then — she stopped. This isn't Tom's handwriting.

prompt: Eleanor holding up the letter, certain expression despite tears, Sophie shocked
subtitle: I knew Tom's handwriting. It was terrible. This is beautiful penmanship. But it's not Tom's.

prompt: Eleanor closing her eyes, remembering — flash of supply crates with elegant labels in a field hospital
subtitle: George. George Whitfield. He labeled every supply crate in the hospital. I'd know that copperplate anywhere.

prompt: Eleanor smiling through tears, telling the story of the night before D-Day, Tom struggling to write
subtitle: The night before D-Day, Tom came to me shaking. Not because of the invasion — because of what he wanted to say.

prompt: Flashback: Tom handing crumpled paper to George in a tent, George writing by candlelight while Tom speaks
subtitle: Tom spoke. George wrote. Every word came from Tom's mouth. George just gave them shape on paper.

prompt: Eleanor holding a magnifying glass over the bottom of the letter, her hands trembling
subtitle: This part — the last two lines — this IS Tom's handwriting. After George finished, Tom took the pen and added something himself.

prompt: Extreme close-up of the smudged lines becoming readable through the magnifying glass: I cant write pretty words But I love you is all I know — Tom
subtitle: I can't write pretty words. But I love you is all I know. — Tom

prompt: Eleanor sobbing quietly, Sophie crying, the letter between them, firelight flickering
subtitle: That was what the ink had hidden for half a century. Just Tom. Raw, clumsy, honest Tom.

prompt: The pocket watch beside the letter, the explanation: he carried it for fifty years because you don't mail a letter to a ghost
subtitle: He never sent it. Because they told him Eleanor was dead. So he folded it into his watch. Every day. Every single day.

prompt: Evening light in Eleanor's sitting room, hours have passed, Sophie asking the difficult question
subtitle: Eleanor, if I had come sooner — would you have wanted to hear his voice?

prompt: Eleanor's passionate response, fifty years of waiting in her voice
subtitle: Wanted to? I've rehearsed what I would say to that man every night for fifty years.

prompt: Sophie breaking the news, Eleanor going completely still, all sound stopping
subtitle: I'm so sorry. He passed away three weeks ago.

prompt: Eleanor staring at the photograph on the wall, devastated, tears falling
subtitle: Fifty years I waited. And I was three weeks too late.

prompt: Eleanor touching the photograph of young Tom with her fingertips, gently, as if it might disappear
subtitle: Every night, I said goodnight to this photograph. I told myself it wasn't foolish. — It wasn't foolish.

prompt: Eleanor at Tom's grave in Pennsylvania, autumn sunset, placing the pocket watch on the headstone
subtitle: I'm here, Tom. I'm finally here.

prompt: Eleanor speaking to the gravestone, tears and a smile, sunset light golden on her face
subtitle: I got your letter. That last line — the one in your terrible beautiful handwriting — that's the finest thing anyone has ever written to me.

prompt: Eleanor laughing at the grave, a small clear laugh like a bell, joy breaking through grief
subtitle: You owe me a kitchen, Thomas Bennett. A kitchen big enough to dance in.

prompt: Eleanor handing Sophie a yellowed sealed envelope by the fireplace
subtitle: I wrote this on June 5th, 1944. The same night he wrote his.

prompt: Close-up of Eleanor's letter: Come back to me Thomas Bennett. I'll be waiting. I'll always be waiting.
subtitle: Come back to me, Thomas Bennett. I don't care if it takes a week or a year. I'll be waiting. I'll always be waiting.

prompt: Two letters placed side by side on a wooden table, his and hers, warm firelight
subtitle: Two letters. Never sent. Never received. And yet — somehow — they had found each other.

prompt: Eleanor sitting in Tom's chair at Christmas with the Bennett family, laughing and telling stories
subtitle: Eleanor spent that Christmas with us. She told us things we never knew — that he sang when he thought no one was listening.

prompt: Eleanor in her armchair in the Cotswolds cottage, peaceful, pocket watch in one hand, letter in the other, photo on the wall above
subtitle: Eleanor passed away the following spring. They found her in the armchair, the pocket watch in one hand, Tom's letter in the other.

prompt: The pocket watch being opened one last time, two letters folded together inside
subtitle: Inside the watch now were two letters. Folded together. Finally together.

prompt: Final shot: the pocket watch ticking, then slowly stopping, fade to black
subtitle: My grandfather couldn't write pretty words. But he loved her. And she waited. And in the end, the letter found its way.
